note: Hand-annotated ground truth for Ioffe & Szegedy 2015 (Batch Normalization). Independent pass.

claims.statement: SGD is effective for training deep networks but requires careful tuning of learning rate and parameter initialization.
Inputs to each layer are affected by parameters of all preceding layers, so small parameter changes amplify with depth.
A shift in the input distribution to a learning system is called covariate shift.
We define Internal Covariate Shift as the change in the distributions of a deep network's internal activations during training.
We propose Batch Normalization, which normalizes each mini-batch's activations by fixing their means and variances, and integrates normalization into the network architecture.
Batch Normalization improves gradient flow by reducing dependence on parameter scale and initial values, enabling much higher learning rates without divergence.
Batch Normalization regularizes the model and reduces the need for Dropout.
Batch Normalization makes saturating nonlinearities trainable by preventing the network from getting stuck in saturated modes.
Applied to the best ImageNet classifier, Batch Normalization reaches equivalent accuracy with only 7% of the original training steps and exceeds it by a substantial margin.
An ensemble of BN-trained networks improves on the best previously known ImageNet classification results.
Batch Normalization introduces two learned parameters per activation — a scale gamma and a shift beta — so the normalized activation can represent the identity.
During inference, the population mean and variance are used in place of mini-batch statistics, making inference deterministic.
For convolutional layers, Batch Normalization shares its parameters across all spatial locations of a given feature map, so that each feature map has a single gamma and beta.
On an MNIST test with sigmoid nonlinearities, the batch-normalized network reaches higher test accuracy and keeps its activation distributions far more stable than the baseline.
BN-Baseline reaches Inception's maximum accuracy in less than half the training steps.
With a 5x higher learning rate, BN-x5 reaches 72.2% accuracy in 14x fewer training steps than Inception.
With 30x higher learning rate, BN-x30 reaches 74.8% accuracy, 5x faster than Inception needed to reach 72.2%.
Without BN, Inception with sigmoid never exceeds chance-level accuracy; with BN it reaches 69.8%.
A BN-Inception ensemble achieves 4.9% top-5 validation error on ImageNet, exceeding the prior best and the estimated human benchmark.
Adding BN lets us remove Dropout from BN-Inception without increasing overfitting.
Local Response Normalization is not necessary in the presence of Batch Normalization.
Batch Normalization is based on extending the notion of covariate shift to sub-networks and layers, and removing that shift from internal activations aids training.
Future work includes applying BN to recurrent neural networks, where internal covariate shift and vanishing/exploding gradients may be especially severe.
We plan to investigate whether BN helps with classical domain adaptation via recomputed population statistics on new data distributions.
